coredata.dc:title: A novel Zn-doped CHA zeolite coupled CDs for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhicheng
Lu, Nan
Cai, Wei
Wen, Jianuan
Li, Kang
Qu, Hongxia
coredata.dc:description: The construction of zeolite/semiconductor was proved to be one of the effective ways to improve the photocatalytic performance. In this paper, CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was prepared by hydrothermal method, and a synergistic interaction was formed between CDs@Zn-CHA and CN to enhance the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. The photoelectric properties characterization and XPS and in situ IR results indicated that CDs, Zn-CHA and CN formed a charge transfer interface, which improved the separation of photogenerated carriers and greatly enhanced the nitrogen fixation efficiency. Nitrogen is adsorbed by Zn-CHA with microporous structure and activated by contact with Zn sites on CHA. CN has a good visible light response and provides photogenerated carriers under visible light irradiation. CDs act on the interfacial charge transfer between Zn-CHA and CN, promoting the transfer of photogenerated electrons to the nitrogen adsorbed by Zn-CHA and accelerating the dissociation of NN, thus increasing the yield of NH3. Compared with CDs@Zn-CHA and CN, 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN achieved 5.82 and 2.99 times of nitrogen fixation yield, respectively. Furthermore, the higher hydrophobicity of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was found by contact angle measurement, which provided more abundant three-phase contact points for the reaction process. This study provides new ideas and directions for the design of photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CHA zeolite
Zn-doping
Carbon dots
g-C3N5
Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Light conversation and interfacial charge transfer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Graduate Research and Innovation Projects of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work were supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant number: 51778296 ) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_0501).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the CDs@Zn-CHA/CN synthesis route.

(a) NH3 yields for different ratio of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, (b) Time-dependent NH3 production at different temperatures, (c) Nitrogen fixation rate of 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN at different temperatures, (d) Cycle stability test.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, (c) HAADF-STEM image and (d–j) Element EDX line scan images of 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of samples.
(a) UV−vis DRS spectra, (b) plots of the transformed Kubelka–Munk function versus photon energy, (c) Band structure alignments for CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, (d) EIS curves of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN and CN.
(a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) Zn 2p XPS spectra of 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN.
(a) Illustration of dynamic and static experiments, (b) CDs@Zn-CHA/CN photocatalytic system.
(a) The local view of In-situ DRIFTS spectra of CDs@Zn-CHA, (b) CN, (c) 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN.
Schematic presentation of photocatalytic NRR by CDs@Zn-CHA/CN.

head.ce:title: A novel Zn-doped CHA zeolite coupled CDs for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of zeolite/semiconductor was proved to be one of the effective ways to improve the photocatalytic performance. In this paper, CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was prepared by hydrothermal method, and a synergistic interaction was formed between CDs@Zn-CHA and CN to enhance the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. The photoelectric properties characterization and XPS and in situ IR results indicated that CDs, Zn-CHA and CN formed a charge transfer interface, which improved the separation of photogenerated carriers and greatly enhanced the nitrogen fixation efficiency. Nitrogen is adsorbed by Zn-CHA with microporous structure and activated by contact with Zn sites on CHA. CN has a good visible light response and provides photogenerated carriers under visible light irradiation. CDs act on the interfacial charge transfer between Zn-CHA and CN, promoting the transfer of photogenerated electrons to the nitrogen adsorbed by Zn-CHA and accelerating the dissociation of NN, thus increasing the yield of NH3. Compared with CDs@Zn-CHA and CN, 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN achieved 5.82 and 2.99 times of nitrogen fixation yield, respectively. Furthermore, the higher hydrophobicity of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was found by contact angle measurement, which provided more abundant three-phase contact points for the reaction process. This study provides new ideas and directions for the design of photocatalyst.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen cycle, dominated by ammonium nitrogen (NH4 +) and nitrate nitrogen (NO3 −), is critical to the global economy and sustaining life [1]. Currently, about 90% of the world's annual NH3 production is achieved primarily through the Haber-Bosch process. However, this route has the disadvantages of harsh production conditions (15–25 Mpa,300–550 °C), environmental pollution and high energy consumption [2–4]. Inspired by biological nitrogen fixation, photocatalytic nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) is recognized as one of the most promising solutions for NH3 production [5–7]. NRR is a new nitrogen fixation process using light as the energy source and nitrogen as the raw material, which has the advantages of simple equipment, low cost and promising future [8–11].

The high bond energy of the NN triple bond of N2 and the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers are two major problems affecting the photocatalytic activity [12–14]. To ameliorate these problems, many schemes have been applied, for example by constructing heterojunctions [15–19], doping [20–24] and defects [25–27]. K.M. Parida's group [27] synthesized MoS2/MIS heterojunction photocatalysts, obtaining high NH3 yields nearly 4 and 7 times higher than bare MoS2 and MIS. However, as a three-phase interfacial reaction, the nitrogen adsorption process of NRR is often neglected [28]. The solubility of nitrogen in water is extremely low (6.9 × 10−4 mol·L−1, 20 °C, 101.325 kPa), and it is vital to ensure that enough nitrogen molecules are adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface to obtain a good nitrogen fixation rate [29,30]. Therefore, improving the adsorption capacity of the catalyst for N2 is also one of the effective ways to improve the nitrogen fixation efficiency. In gas adsorption, zeolite with rich porous structure and large specific surface area show a unique charm [31–33]. When the diameter of gas molecules is close to the pore diameter of porous materials, the diffusion direction of molecules will be guided and the diffusion energy barrier drops sharply, thus achieving efficient adsorption of gas molecules [34,35]. Graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is considered to be an effective photocatalyst due to its unique chemical structure and optical properties (excellent visible light absorption ability and thermal stability) [36]. However, g-C3N4 is not ideal for nitrogen fixation due to its disadvantages such as easy complexation of photogenerated holes and electrons and small specific surface area [37]. Compared with g-C3N4, the new porous carbon nitride material g-C3N5 has a narrower energy band gap and better solar absorption efficiency due to its larger π-conjugation network [38–40]. Therefore g-C3N5 has good potential for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation applications [41]. However, its nitrogen fixation efficiency is limited due to its lack of active sites and low activity for N2 adsorption [42]. The pore diameter of CHA molecular sieve (3.8–4.3 Å) is close to the molecular diameter of N2 (3.4 Å), which has a strong adsorption capacity for N2 [43–45]. In addition, the strong electric field strength between the aluminosilicate framework and non-framework cations of CHA can inhibit the recombination of e−/h+ pairs and promote the effective charge separation and transfer [46–48]. Therefore, CHA has good potential for NRR.

Nevertheless, CHA suffers from the lack of active sites and poor light absorption ability, leading to less research on their individual application in photocatalysis [48]. As an emerging light-emitting nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most promising materials in the field of photocatalysis due to excellent photocatalytic properties [49–53]. In photocatalysis, CDs are often used as (I) electron mediators to facilitate interfacial electron transfer and (II) photosensitizers to utilize more visible light [54,55]. Liu's group synthesized CDs@Fe2O3 composites and demonstrated that CDs achieve effective interfacial electron transfer, thereby increasing photocatalytic hydrogen production in water [56]. Herein, CDs were introduced onto CHA and acted as a transfer of photogenerated electrons generated by CN to Zn-CHA, which enhanced the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation efficiency [57].

In this paper, CDs@Zn-CHA was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal route, and CDs were generated by organic template agent N-Methyl piperidine (NMP) under the guidance of zinc ions and coupled within the CHA molecular sieve at high temperature. CDs@Zn-CHA was loaded on g-C3N5 (CN). Compared with g-C3N5, CDs@Zn-CHA/CN showed higher nitrogen adsorption capacity and photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. To clarify the role of CDs@Zn-CHA and the reasons for the improvement of nitrogen fixing activity, XPS, FTIR, Contact angle and EIS experiments were carried out. The higher ammonia yield of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN indicates that loading CDs on zeolite molecular sieve is a feasible way to improve the photocatalytic nitrogen fixing effect, and provides a new idea for the modification of photocatalytic nitrogen fixing catalyst.

A certain amount of zinc acetate dihydrate and aluminum isopropoxide was dissolved in 14.6 mL (14 mL of deionized water and 0.6 mL of phosphoric acid solution) of mixed solution and stirred for 1 h. Add 4.2 mL of NMP (N-methylpiperidine) to the mixture and stir for 2 h to obtain a white homogeneous solution. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL teflon lined autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 72 h, and centrifuged at 4200 rpm to obtain a white CDs@Zn-CHA sample.

The g-C3N4 was prepared by a typical pyrolysis of dicyandiamide in air atmosphere. The detailed preparation steps were as follows: 10 g dicyandiamide were put in a Muffle Furnace and heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min and calcined at 500 °C for 4 h.
In the typical synthesis, 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole was calcined at 500 °C for 3 h under an air atmosphere. A brown product, g-C3N5 (CN) was obtained after naturally cool to room temperature.

200 mg of CN was added in 15 mL of ethanol, and ultrasonically dispersed for 2 h. Then, 20 mg of CDs@Zn-CHA was dispersed in the above mixture and sonicated for 1 h. The crystallized at 120 °C in Teflon stainless-steel autoclaves for 12 h. After centrifugation, the obtained solid was dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and the sample obtained was 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN. Based on different CDs@Zn-CHA:CN feed mass, it got 5%, 10%,15%, 20% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN. The complete preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 .

The photocatalytic N2 fixation was conducted in a 100 mL jacketed reactor with an external cooling water system to kept cool at 35 °C. Briefly, 50 mg of the catalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of 5 wt% aqueous methanol solution, and purity nitrogen gas was bubbled into the reaction solution with a flow rate of 30 mL min-1. After stirring for 30 min in the dark, a 300 W Xe lamp was turned on. The Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300/300UV, working current 15 A, output light wavelength 200–2500 nm) was used to as a solar simulator. Take 2 mL of reaction solution every 5 min and centrifuge for 10 min (4200 rpm). The concentration of ammonia was determined by Nessler's reagent method (JB7478–87) with UV–visible spectrophotometer (T6 new century). The ammonia concentration was calculated by ammonia nitrogen standard curve (Fig. S2).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN instrument with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a Nicolet-iS10 spectrometer (32 scans). The working instrument model for XRD was a Purkinje XD-3 (35 kV, 20 mA, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å). The instrument for UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) spectroscopy was a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected at a PHI QUANTERA II photoelectron spectrometer (Al Kα X-ray radiation) and the carbon correction value (CC) was 284.8 eV. The temperature-controlled desorption (TPD) measurement instrument model is ChemiSorb 2040.

The photocatalytic activity of the x%-CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (x = 5%,10%,15%,20%,25%) were examined under light irradiation at 35 °C for 4 h. In Fig. 1a , the NH3 yield of 10% CDs/Zn-CHA/CN was 444.48 μmol·h−1·g−1, which is 5.82 and 2.99 times more than that of CDs@Zn-CHA (76.33 μmol·h−1·g−1) and CN (148.6 μmol·h−1·g−1), respectively. According to Eq.S1, the apparent conversion efficiency (solar to chemical conversion efficiency) was calculated to be 0.07%. The ammonia yield of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN decreases with the increasing loading of CDs@Zn-CHA from 15% to 20%, which is probably due to the reduction of the light absorption capacity of the composite when the amount of CDs@Zn-CHA is too large. Under the Ar atmosphere (Fig. 1c), the yield of NH3 is almost zero, indicating that the nitrogen element in the catalyst does not participate in the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction. In addition, the isotopic labeling experiments were carried out by indophenol blue method and the products were analyzed by HPLC-MS [58]. In Fig. S11(a, b), the peak at m/z = 198 is higher relative to that at m/z = 199 when the reactant is 14N2, while the opposite is true when the reactant is 15N2 [59]. These results confirm that the produced NH4 + indeed originates from photocatalytic N2 fixation.

Temperature is one of the important factors affecting the rate of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. In Fig. 1(b, c), in the range of 15–45 °C, the nitrogen fixation rate increased with temperature, but when the temperature increased to 55 °C, the rate decreased instead. The reason for this is that in multiphase reaction, the increase in temperature leads to a decrease in the degree of N2 adsorption on the catalyst. The gas is rapidly desorbed after being adsorbed by the solid, resulting in fewer reactants available for chemical nitrogen fixation and a lower reaction rate. Kinetic analysis was performed on 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN at a nitrogen flow rate of 30 mL/min to deduce the number of steps of the reaction and the reaction activation energy
E a
.

a

RT
)
where
r (
N
H 3
)
is the NH3 production rate (μmol·h−1·g−1), A is the antecedent factor, which is calculated to have a value of 1.01×106 (μmol·h−1·g−1). R is the gas constant,
T
is the reaction temperature (289,299,309,319,329 K), and

are the reaction rate constants. In this reaction, the concentrations of H2O and N2 do not change, so Eq. (2) can be simplified to Eq. (3). It is observed that the reaction is a typical zero-order reaction. The relationship between the temperature T and the value of
r (
N
H 3
)
follows the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (4)). As shown in Fig. S4, the Ea of the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction on the CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was calculated to be 14.03 kJ·mol–1, which is lower than the reported NRR catalysts (40–50 kJ mol−1) [59,60]. Thus, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation application of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN deserve further study.

The results of cycling stability of 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN are shown in Fig. 1d. After four photocatalytic nitrogen fixation experiments, the NH3 yield still maintains more than 90%, indicating that CDs@Zn-CHA/CN has good stability and recyclability.
In order to reveal the mechanism of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, the morphology, structure, properties and active center were investigated.

The heterogeneous interface between CN and CDs@Zn-CHA and the embedding of CDs in Zn-CHA can be clearly observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 2 a) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Fig. 2b). In Fig. 2(a, b), the particle size of CDs ranges from 3 to 15 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm corresponding to the (100) face of graphitic carbon (inserted in Fig. 2b) [61,62]. The TEM-FFT images in Fig. S1(a, b) correspond to the (0 1 2), (0 2 1), (0 1 3), (0 3 0) planes of CHA and the (0 0 1), (0 1 0), (0 1 1) planes of CN, which demonstrates CDs@Zn-CHA was successfully loaded on the CN by hydrothermal method.

The microstructure and compositional distribution of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN were further investigated by HAADF-STEM and EDX line scan. EDX plots show the presence of C, N, O, P, Al and Zn with clear separation (Fig. 2c–j), confirming the successful complexation of CDs and Zn-CHA/CN.

The structure and functional groups of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN were further characterized by XRD and FT-IR, respectively. In Fig. 3 a, the diffraction peaks (9.4°, 13.6°, 15.9°, 18.7°, 20.5°, 25.8°, 30.9°) in CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, corresponding to the (1 0 1), (0 1 2), (0 2 1), (2 0 2), (2 1 1), (2 2 1), (2 2 0), (2 1 4) planes of CHA molecular sieves (JCPDS No. 53-1037). The characteristic peaks at 13.1°corresponding to the (1 0 0) related to triazine structure and the characteristic peaks of interlayer stacking of aromatics 27.5° corresponding to the (0 0 2) planes of CN. Fig. S5a shows that the peak for (1 0 0) face of CN decreases from 13.4° to 13.0° as the CDs@Zn-CHA content increases. The Van der Waals forces between CDs and CN destabilize the triazine structure [63], distorting the lattice of CN and leading to a left shift of the diffraction peak. The characteristic peaks of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN did not change significantly in the XRD patterns before and after the reaction (Fig. S5b), which proved the high stability of the material structure.

In the infrared spectrum, for CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (Fig. 3b), the broad band from 3500 to 3000 cm−1 is generated by NH vibrations [64,65]. A series of characteristic peaks such as 1635, 1573, 1420, 1332 and 1250 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic stretching modes of CN and CN in CN. Significant bending vibrations of the tris-triazine unit can be observed at 810∼900 cm−1. The 1100 cm−1 region belongs to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the zeolite OTO tetrahedron. Fig. S6 shows the FT-IR plots of the composites at different CDs@Zn-CHA introductions. The peak intensities of a series of characteristic bands appearing at 1700–1200 cm−1 for CN and CN decrease with increasing CDs@Zn-CHA content, proving that CDs affect the stability of the triazine structure of CN.

To investigate the specific surface area and pore size distribution of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, BET tests were performed. Fig. S7a shows the type IV adsorption isotherm with H4 hysteresis loop for CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, indicating the presence of mesoporous and micropore structures in the sample [66]. Furthermore, the pore size distribution shown in Fig. S7b also demonstrates the presence of plenty of mesoporous (15–40 nm) and microporous (<1 nm) structures in CDs@Zn-CHA/CN [67]. Temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) characterized the adsorption and activation of CDs@Zn-CHA and CDs@Zn-CHA/CN for N2 (Fig. S3). CDs@Zn-CHA shows a desorption peak at 425 °C related to chemisorsobed N2, indicating strong interaction of CHA channels with N2 molecules. The weaker peak at 81 °C is related to the physical adsorption of N2. After combining with CN, both peaks shift to higher temperatures of 90 °C and 433 °C, indicating that the N2 capture and activation ability of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was enhanced. At the same time, the absorption peak intensity of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN is significantly stronger than that of CDs@Zn-CHA, which indicates that the increase of chemisorption sites will be beneficial to nitrogen adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light conversation and interfacial charge transfer
ce:section.ce:para: Figure 4 a shows the UV–vis DRS plots of CN, CDs@Zn-CHA and x%CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (x = 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25%). The light absorption of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN is significantly enhanced in the UV and visible regions compared to CDs@Zn-CHA, which verifies that the synergistic interaction between CN and CDs@Zn-CHA improves the light absorption efficiency. The CDs@Zn-CHA/CN light absorption edges were clearly red-shifted due to the conjugated groups generated between the CDs and CN on the surface of CDs@Zn-CHA. As shown in Fig.4b, by the Kubelka-Munk formula, the band gaps of x% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (x = 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25%) are 2.69, 2.63, 2.63, 2.64 and 2.66 eV, respectively. The narrowing of the band gap makes it easier for the electrons to transition from the valence band to the conduction band [22], so 10% and 15%CDs@Zn-CHA/CN with smallest band gap have the strongest optical responsiveness.

To further determine the band structure of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, we performed a Mott-Schottky test to obtain its flat-band potential (Ec). The electrode potentials (EAg/AgCl) were converted to NHE scale (ENHE) at pH = 7 by using Eq. (5) given below:
(5)
E NHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+
E
Ag / Cl
0
− 0.0591 ·
(
7 − pH
)

E
Ag / AgCl
0
= 0.197
V
at 25℃. As presented in Fig. S8a, by Eq. (5), the ENHE values of x%CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (x = 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25%) are −0.31, −0.48, −0.35, −0.22 and −0.18 eV, respectively. Finally, according to the equation Ec (ENHE) = Ev – Eg (Fig.4c), the valence band Ev for x%CDs@Zn-CHA/CN was calculated as 2.38, 2.15, 2.28, 2.42 and 2.48 eV, respectively. The larger forbidden band size and valence band demonstrate that the complexation of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN decreases the complexation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers generated by CN and increases the photocatalytic quantum utilization efficiency. The separation efficiency of the photon generated carriers can be illustrated by the EIS. As shown in Fig. 4d, the curve radius of all CDs@Zn-CHA/CN is significantly smaller than that of CN, which can be attributed to the fact that CDs transferred the photogenerated electrons of CN to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers of the catalysts [49,50]. It can be seen from the transient photocurrent response plot (Fig. S8b) that CDs@Zn-CHA/CN exhibits a stronger transient photocurrent response than CN and CHA. The above results indicate that the ternary composite structure of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN can significantly improve the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and the interfacial charge transfer capability.

XPS was used to detect the chemical environment changes of 10%CDs@Zn-CHA/CN before and after the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction. As shown in Fig. 5 (a-c), for the C 1s spectra of fresh and used 10%CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, the observed binding energies of 288.2, 286.04 and 284.83 eV correspond to NCN2, CNH and CC, respectively [42,68]. For N 1s, belonging to CNNC (400.81 eV), N3C (399.34 eV) and N2C (398.54 eV) peaks appear in fresh and used 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN [69,70]. The O 1s spectra are decomposed into two peaks with binding energy of 532.79 eV and 531.83 eV attributed to CO and CO/PO [62]. Figure 5(b, d) show that the peak of Zn 2p3/2 shifts significantly (1045.19 eV → 1045.65 eV) and the peak of ZnN shifts from 397.37 eV to 396.88 eV [71], while the binding energies of the other elements do not change significantly, indicating that electrons migrate from Zn to N. As Zn of CDs@Zn-CHA keeps transferring photogenerated electrons, the adsorbed N2 gains electrons, weakening the high bonding energy of NN. Thus, it proves that Zn is the activation center of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN.

To characterize the hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of the samples, contact angle tests were performed on CDs@Zn-CHA, CN and 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. S10(a-c) that the contact angle of 10% CDs@Zn-CHA/CN is 48.97°, which is higher than that of CN (46.94°) and CDs@Zn-CHA (32.58°). The composite of CDs@Zn-CHA and CN increases the hydrophobicity of the material, and the high hydrophobicity of the material allows more N2 to directly contact the active sites of the material, so that the catalyst can better adsorb and activate gas-phase nitrogen. The above conclusions are in line with the performance of the material in the nitrogen fixation reaction, according to previous studies and Fig. S9, both dissolved nitrogen and gaseous nitrogen exist in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction, and the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation effect of gaseous nitrogen is better than that of dissolved nitrogen [28,29]. Based on the above results, the hydrophilic and hydrophobic structure of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN can form a three-phase interface, which accelerates the proportion of gaseous nitrogen adsorbed by the catalyst, improving the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance (Fig. 6 a,b).

To further investigate the mechanism of chemisorption of nitrogen active sites on composite CDs@Zn-CHA/CN and the activation of hydrogen in water, in situ IR measurements of NRR were performed for CDs@Zn-CHA (Fig. 7 a), CN (Fig. 7b) and CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (Fig. 7c). For CDs@Zn-CHA/CN, the characteristic absorption peaks at 3540 and 1140 cm−1 were attributed to the NH and NH2-NH2 group characteristic peaks, respectively, which are the transition states of N2 to NH4 + in the nitrogen fixation reaction [57]. Compared with CN, the NH4 + characteristic absorption peak and the chemisorption peak of N2 out of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN at 1360 cm−1 are stronger and the peak variation is more obvious, which indicates that N2 is mainly activated by the adsorption of CDs@Zn-CHA. In Fig. 4(a, b), CN with higher light absorption capacity has a stronger ability to generate photoelectron-hole pairs, which are responsible for providing charge carriers to split water to generate oxygen and protonated hydrogen gas. The NH and NH2-NH2 intermediates are significantly stronger than CN at the CDs@Zn-CHA/CN peak intensity, suggesting that the proton hydrogen and electrons activated on CN are transferred to CDs@Zn-CHA to react with NN in the activated state. During NRR, the absorption intensity of the above characteristic peak increases with the reaction, indicating that the reaction of immobilizing N2 and converting it to NH4 + continues.

Based on the above experimental results, a possible reaction mechanism was proposed. As shown in Eq. (6), CDs@Zn-CHA adsorbed both dissolved and gaseous nitrogen, and N2 formed ZnN bonds with Zn. Upon absorption of light irradiation, electron-hole pairs are generated on the surface of g-C3N5 and water is decomposed into protonated hydrogen and oxygen via (Eqs. (7), (8)). As shown in Eq. (9), the electrons generated by g-C3N5 come to Zn-CHA via CDs to further activate the subsequent reduction. The transferred electrons with proton hydrogen are transferred to the activated N2 to produce NHNH, NH2-NH2 and NH3 as shown in (Eqs. (10)–(13)). All the above reaction processes on CDs@Zn-CHA/CN can be represented in Fig. 8 .
(6)

+
+ 2
e
−
→
N
H 2
− N
H 2
· · · Zn −
CHA
(13)
N
H 2
− N
H 2
· · · Zn − CHA + 4
H
+
+ 2
e
−
→
2
NH 4 +
+
Zn
− CHA

In conclusion, CDs@Zn-CHA/CN with high efficiency of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation were prepared by hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN (10%) was significantly improved (474.05 μmol·h−1·g−1) compared to that of CDs@Zn-CHA (76.33 μmol·h−1·g−1) and C3N5 (148.6 μmol·h−1·g−1). The excellent photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance is attributed to the mutual support of the CDs@Zn-CHA and CN structures. The pore homogeneous Zn-CHA has a strong nitrogen adsorption capacity and provides a large number of Zn sites for the dissociation of NN. CN nanosheets with good light absorption properties as substrates can provide photogenerated carriers for photocatalytic reactions, while the introduction of CDs effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated holes and electrons, which transfers the photogenerated electrons generated by CN to Zn active sites to accelerate the NN dissociation rate. It shows that low CDs@Zn-CHA dosage leads to low chemisorption sites and high CDs@Zn-CHA dosage leads to fast nitrogen desorption, and the optimal mass ratio of CDs@Zn-CHA dosage to CN dosage is 10%. The hydrophilic and hydrophobic structures of CDs@Zn-CHA/CN can form a three-phase interface to accelerate the ratio of gaseous nitrogen adsorption by the catalyst and further improve the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance. This work provides a feasible mechanism for CDs@Zn-CHA/CN photocatalytic nitrogen fixation and deepens the understanding of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation.

Zhicheng Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Nan Lu: Validation, Visualization, Data curation. Wei Cai: Investigation, Resources. Jianuan Wen: Investigation, Visualization. Kang Li: Methodology, Validation. Hongxia Qu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work were supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant number: 51778296) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_0501).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mcat.2023.113471.
